metadata.text: 20IMCAT422- OB_ Module 2 1 Dr. Sunandha Rajagopal Organizational Behaviour Module 2- Theory of Behaviour and Culture Introduction Every organization exists within a broader cultural context, and at the same time, it develops its own internal culture. Culture shapes how people think, behave, and interact—both inside and outside the workplace. According to Jerald Greenberg, “Culture provides the social and psychological environment in which organizational behaviour occurs. It influences what people value, how they communicate, and how they respond to authority, change, and teamwork.” In simple terms, culture is the set of shared beliefs, values, norms, and practices that guide how members of a society or organization behave. Meaning of Culture The word culture comes from the Latin term “colere”, meaning to cultivate or to grow. In organizational terms, culture refers to the collective mindset that shapes the attitudes and behaviors of people at work. Definition: According to Edgar Schein, “Culture is a pattern of shared basic assumptions that a group learns as it solves its problems of external adaptation and internal integration.” Fred Luthans defines culture as “The acquired knowledge that people use to interpret experiences and generate social behavior.” In short, culture determines “the way things are done” in a particular group or organization. Levels of Culture (Edgar Schein’s Model) Culture operates at multiple levels: 20IMCAT422- OB_ Module 2 2 Dr. Sunandha Rajagopal 1- Artefacts. These describe any overt, visible, describable aspects of the organisation. Think things like branding and logos, office design, dress code, policies and tools. Things that you can see. 2- Espoused values. This is how people would describe the organisation, in current or aspirational terms. These include missions, goals, value statements, and social contracts. 3- Underlying assumptions. These are unconscious, unspoken, hard to articulate elements of the organisation, particularly from within. They include the “real” but unspoken aim of the organisation (to make the owner or shareholders wealthy? To serve the public? To create jobs?) and underlying beliefs about quality, speed, or safety. Dimensions of Culture (Hofstede’s Model) Geert Hofstede identified six cultural dimensions that explain how values vary across societies. These dimensions help understand how culture influences organizational behaviour. DimensionExplanationExamples / OB Implications 1. Power DistanceThe extent to which inequality and hierarchy are accepted. High power distance cultures (India, China) respect authority; Low power distance cultures (USA, Denmark) prefer equality. 2. Individualism vs. Collectivism Focus on individual goals vs. group harmony. Individualistic cultures encourage personal achievement; collectivist cultures emphasize teamwork. 3. Masculinity vs. Femininity Emphasis on competitiveness vs. care and cooperation. Masculine cultures (Japan, USA) value performance; feminine cultures (Sweden, Norway) value quality of life. 4. Uncertainty Avoidance Comfort with ambiguity and risk. High uncertainty avoidance (France, Japan) prefers rules and structure; low (India, USA) accepts flexibility. 5. Long-term vs. Short-term Orientation Focus on future goals vs. immediate results. Asian cultures emphasize long-term success; Western cultures prefer quick outcomes. 6. Indulgence vs. Restraint Allowing gratification of desires vs. strict social control. Indulgent cultures (USA, Australia) value enjoyment; restrained ones (China, Russia) value discipline.

(India, USA) accepts flexibility. 5. Long-term vs. Short-term Orientation Focus on future goals vs. immediate results. Asian cultures emphasize long-term success; Western cultures prefer quick outcomes. 6. Indulgence vs. Restraint Allowing gratification of desires vs. strict social control. Indulgent cultures (USA, Australia) value enjoyment; restrained ones (China, Russia) value discipline. Types of Organizational Culture 1. Hierarchical Culture: Hierarchical Culture can be characterized by the rigid organizational structure where decision-making is centralized, communication goes from the top down, and strict adherence to the prescribed procedure is encouraged. The power is concentrated in the hands of the most senior leaders. This can provide the stability and efficiency needed to compete in some markets, but it can be very inflexible. 2. Clan Culture: A clan culture involves the organization functioning as an extended family. A clan culture is one in which employees work together as they support one another with a touch of a feeling of belongingness. The leaders work as coaches as they promote the spirit of open communication and trust. Members of the culture are motivated as they feel that they are being appreciated by their leaders. 20IMCAT422- OB_ Module 2 3 Dr. Sunandha Rajagopal 3. Adhocracy Culture: Adhocracy Culture is a kind of corporate culture that focuses on innovation, flexibility, and creativity. It is a dynamic and entrepreneurial culture, typical for organizations that operate in a stable environment with little hierarchy. Adhocracy Culture is characterized by a dynamic and entrepreneurial place where employees are encouraged to take risks for the benefit of the organization. This culture is focused on external flexibility, with employees encouraged to challenge the prevailing situation and innovate. 4. Market Culture: A market culture is associated with the organization’s focus on competitiveness, results, and goal achievement. In a market culture, organizations have a strong external orientation and are actively focused on meeting needs and exceeding competitors. Employees are motivated by metrics, goals, and market share, and a strong emphasis is put on their accountability. 5. Bureaucratic Culture: Bureaucratic Culture is a term that is used to refer to a type of organizational culture that is characterized by formalized procedures or rules, hierarchical structures and adherence to rules and regulations. In a bureaucratic culture, the one who has the power to make decisions is the management and the lower employees are bound up by the protocol and the process set. 6. Innovative Culture: Innovative Culture is a work environment designed to promote creativity, experimentation, and the provision of new ideas. This type of culture allows employees to think outside the box, take risks, and deviate from the existing conditions. It is focused on the development of collaborative practices and an open-minded approach to multiple views. Influence of Culture on Organizational Behaviour Culture has a powerful impact on how employees think, act, and interact in organizations. It affects everything from communication and leadership to motivation and decision-making. 1. Influence on Communication  Language, tone, gestures, and communication style differ across cultures.  In high-context cultures (Japan, Arab nations), meaning

on Organizational Behaviour Culture has a powerful impact on how employees think, act, and interact in organizations. It affects everything from communication and leadership to motivation and decision-making. 1. Influence on Communication  Language, tone, gestures, and communication style differ across cultures.  In high-context cultures (Japan, Arab nations), meaning is derived from non-verbal cues and relationships.  In low-context cultures (US, Germany), communication is direct and explicit. 2. Influence on Motivation  In individualistic cultures, rewards like bonuses or promotions motivate employees.  In collectivist cultures, group rewards, recognition, and social approval are stronger motivators.  For example, in Japan, teamwork and belonging motivate employees more than individual competition. 3. Influence on Leadership  Leadership styles vary based on cultural expectations.  In high power-distance cultures, employees expect autocratic or paternal leadership.  In low power-distance cultures, participative and democratic leadership is preferred. 4. Influence on Decision-Making  In collectivist cultures, decisions are made through group consensus.  In individualistic cultures, quick and independent decision-making is valued. 5. Influence on Conflict Management  Western cultures are more open to confrontation and debate. 20IMCAT422- OB_ Module 2 4 Dr. Sunandha Rajagopal  Eastern cultures prefer harmony and indirect resolution methods. 6. Influence on Organizational Structure  Cultures that value order and control often have rigid hierarchies.  Cultures that value flexibility adopt flatter, decentralized structures. 7. Influence on Ethics and Social Responsibility  Cultural beliefs shape attitudes toward honesty, fairness, and social behavior.  For example, gift-giving in business is common in Asian cultures but may be viewed as bribery in Western ones. 8. Influence on Work-Life Balance  Some cultures (like Europe) prioritize personal time and work-life balance.  Others (like the U.S. or Japan) expect long working hours and high performance. Positive and Negative Effects of Culture on OB Positive InfluenceNegative Influence Promotes shared identity and belonging. Can create resistance to change. Builds cooperation and teamwork.May cause cultural clashes in global teams. Provides stability and predictability.Overemphasis on conformity may suppress creativity. Guides ethical and responsible behaviour. Misalignment between national and corporate culture can cause conflict. Managing Cultural Differences in Organizations To handle cultural diversity effectively, managers and employees should: 1.Develop Cultural Awareness – Understand and respect differences in beliefs, customs, and communication styles. 2.Provide Cross-Cultural Training – Train employees in global etiquette and communication practices. 3.Encourage Inclusion and Diversity – Promote equality and respect for all backgrounds. 4.Adopt Flexible Leadership Styles – Adjust leadership and motivation strategies according to cultural expectations. 5.Foster Open Communication – Build trust and transparency across diverse teams. 6.Build a Strong Organizational Culture – Create shared values that unite employees beyond national or ethnic differences. Examples  Google promotes a culture of openness and innovation where employees from different nationalities share ideas freely. 20IMCAT422- OB_ Module 2 5 Dr. Sunandha Rajagopal  Toyota blends Japanese values of teamwork and quality with global diversity.  Infosys in India encourages both traditional respect for hierarchy and modern collaboration through its training programs. Personality – Meaning, Formation,

innovation where employees from different nationalities share ideas freely. 20IMCAT422- OB_ Module 2 5 Dr. Sunandha Rajagopal  Toyota blends Japanese values of teamwork and quality with global diversity.  Infosys in India encourages both traditional respect for hierarchy and modern collaboration through its training programs. Personality – Meaning, Formation, Determinants, Traits, and Influence in Organizational Behaviour 1. Meaning of Personality The word personality is derived from the Latin term “persona”, meaning mask—the masks actors used to wear in ancient theatre. In psychology and organizational behaviour, personality refers to the unique and stable pattern of thoughts, feelings, and behaviors that make one individual different from another. Stephen P. Robbins defines personality as: “The sum total of ways in which an individual reacts to and interacts with others.” Jerald Greenberg describes it as: “The set of psychological characteristics that influence an individual’s consistent patterns of behavior.” In simple terms: Personality is what makes you you—how you think, feel, and act in various situations. 2. Formation of Personality Personality develops over time through a combination of biological, psychological, and environmental factors. It is not something we are born with entirely—it evolves as we grow and interact with the world. Key Theories of Personality Formation: 1.Psychoanalytic Theory (Sigmund Freud) The psychoanalytic theory explains personality as the result of unconscious motives and inner conflicts. According to Freud, human personality consists of three parts: id, ego, and superego. The id operates on the pleasure principle and seeks immediate satisfaction of basic instincts. The ego works on the reality principle and balances the demands of the id with real-world situations. The superego represents moral values and social norms. Freud emphasized that early childhood experiences strongly influence personality development. Unresolved conflicts during childhood may lead to emotional problems and shape adult behaviour. 2.Trait Theory (Allport, Cattell, Eysenck) Trait theory focuses on identifying stable and measurable traits that describe personality. Traits are consistent patterns of behaviour, thoughts, and emotions. Allport classified traits into cardinal, central, and secondary traits. Cattell identified 16 personality factors using statistical analysis. Eysenck proposed three major dimensions: introversion–extroversion, neuroticism, and psychoticism. According to this theory, individuals differ in the degree to which they possess these traits. Trait theory helps in predicting behaviour and understanding individual differences. 3.Social Learning Theory (Bandura) Social learning theory explains personality development through observation, imitation, and reinforcement. Bandura stated that people learn behaviours by watching others, especially role models such as parents and teachers. This process is called observational learning. Rewards and punishments influence whether a behaviour is repeated. The theory also introduces the concept of self-efficacy, which 20IMCAT422- OB_ Module 2 6 Dr. Sunandha Rajagopal refers to a person’s belief in their ability to succeed. Social learning theory emphasizes the role of environmental factors along with personal experiences in shaping personality. 4.Humanistic Theory (Maslow, Rogers) Humanistic theory views personality as a result of personal growth and self-development. Maslow proposed a hierarchy of needs, where basic needs must be satisfied before higher needs, leading to self- actualization. Rogers emphasized the

the role of environmental factors along with personal experiences in shaping personality. 4.Humanistic Theory (Maslow, Rogers) Humanistic theory views personality as a result of personal growth and self-development. Maslow proposed a hierarchy of needs, where basic needs must be satisfied before higher needs, leading to self- actualization. Rogers emphasized the importance of self-concept and positive self-regard. According to this theory, individuals have free will and naturally strive to achieve their full potential. A supportive and accepting environment helps in healthy personality development. The humanistic approach focuses on positive human qualities rather than weaknesses. 5.Interactionist Approach (Greenberg & Luthans) The interactionist approach explains personality as the result of the interaction between heredity and environment. It suggests that behaviour is influenced by both personal traits and situational factors. People may behave differently in different situations, even with the same personality traits. This approach rejects the idea that personality alone determines behaviour. Instead, it highlights how situational context affects actions. The interactionist approach is widely used in organizational behaviour to understand employee performance and workplace behaviour. 3. Determinants of Personality Personality is determined by several interrelated factors: a) Heredity (Genetic Factors)  Heredity determines basic personality structure such as temperament, intelligence, and biological rhythms.  Traits like energy level, emotional reactivity, and shyness may be inherited.  Example: Identical twin studies show strong similarities even when raised apart. b) Environment  Includes family background, culture, education, peer influence, and life experiences.  Family and social environment shape attitudes, values, and behaviour.  Example: A supportive family encourages confidence and sociability. c) Situation  Personality can change slightly depending on context.  A person may be dominant at work but quiet at home.  Situational demands influence how personality traits are expressed. d) Culture  Culture determines acceptable and expected behaviours.  For instance, collectivist cultures emphasize cooperation, while individualistic cultures value independence. e) Learning and Life Experiences  Positive and negative experiences reinforce certain behaviours, gradually shaping personality. 20IMCAT422- OB_ Module 2 7 Dr. Sunandha Rajagopal 4. Major Traits of Personality Traits are enduring characteristics that describe an individual’s behavior. Modern personality research identifies five broad traits known as the Big Five Personality Model, widely used in organizational behaviour. TraitDescriptionOB Relevance 1. ExtraversionOutgoing, sociable, energetic, and assertive. Extraverts perform well in jobs requiring teamwork, leadership, and social interaction. 2. AgreeablenessCooperative, trusting, kind, and compassionate. High agreeableness improves teamwork and reduces conflict. 3. ConscientiousnessResponsible, dependable, organized, and goal-oriented. Most strongly linked with job performance, reliability, and persistence. 4. Emotional Stability (Neuroticism) Calm, secure, and confident vs. anxious, moody, and insecure. Emotionally stable people handle stress better and show higher satisfaction. 5. Openness to Experience Curious, imaginative, creative, and flexible. Encourages innovation, adaptability, and learning. Jerald Greenberg also notes that these “Big Five” traits predict success in various work roles and are useful in employee selection, leadership, and motivation. 5. Personality Attributes Influencing Organizational Behaviour Certain specific personality attributes directly affect workplace behaviour, performance, leadership, and teamwork. AttributeMeaningInfluence on OB 1. Locus of ControlDegree to

Jerald Greenberg also notes that these “Big Five” traits predict success in various work roles and are useful in employee selection, leadership, and motivation. 5. Personality Attributes Influencing Organizational Behaviour Certain specific personality attributes directly affect workplace behaviour, performance, leadership, and teamwork. AttributeMeaningInfluence on OB 1. Locus of ControlDegree to which individuals believe they control their fate. • Internal: Believe they control outcomes. • External: Believe fate or luck controls outcomes. Internals take responsibility, perform better, and are more motivated. Externals rely on supervision. 2. MachiavellianismManipulative behaviour; focuses on self-interest and control over others. High “Mach” employees are persuasive but may harm trust and ethics. 3. Self-EsteemThe degree to which individuals like or dislike themselves. High self-esteem leads to confidence and risk- taking; low self-esteem leads to conformity and dependence. 4. Self-MonitoringAbility to adjust behaviour to external situations. High self-monitors adapt easily and perform well in social and managerial roles. 5. Risk-Taking Propensity Willingness to take or avoid risks. High risk-takers make quick decisions; useful in entrepreneurship but may lead to errors. 6. Type A and Type B Personality • Type A: Competitive, impatient, aggressive. • Type B: Relaxed, patient, easy- Type A people excel in fast-paced environments but face stress. Type B show steady, cooperative performance. 20IMCAT422- OB_ Module 2 8 Dr. Sunandha Rajagopal going. 7. Proactive Personality Tendency to take initiative and change the environment. Proactive individuals are innovative, adaptable, and valuable for organizational change. 8. AuthoritarianismBelief in obedience and power hierarchy. High authoritarianism suits structured jobs; low suits creative roles. 9. NarcissismExcessive self-focus and need for admiration. Can boost confidence and leadership visibility but may harm teamwork. 10. Emotional Intelligence (EI) Ability to perceive, understand, and manage emotions—one’s own and others’. High EI leads to better leadership, teamwork, and conflict management. 6. Personality and Organizational Behaviour Personality strongly influences how people behave, interact, and perform in organizations. a) Job Performance  Conscientious and emotionally stable employees show higher productivity.  Extraverts perform better in customer-facing or leadership roles. b) Job Satisfaction  People with positive personalities (optimism, emotional stability) are more satisfied with their jobs. c) Leadership Effectiveness  Leaders with high self-confidence, conscientiousness, and emotional intelligence inspire better performance from teams. d) Interpersonal Relations  Agreeable and emotionally intelligent employees maintain healthier relationships and resolve conflicts effectively. e) Motivation and Commitment  Internals (high locus of control) and proactive individuals are more self-motivated and committed to goals. f) Stress Management  People with emotional stability and high EI handle pressure and workplace stress effectively. 7. Personality and Fit in the Organization Personality influences how well a person fits with their job, team, and organization. 1.Person–Job Fit: Matching personality with job requirements improves satisfaction and performance. (e.g., Extroverts thrive in sales; introverts in analytical roles.) 2.Person–Organization Fit: 20IMCAT422- OB_ Module 2 9 Dr. Sunandha Rajagopal When an individual’s values align with the organization’s culture, they show stronger commitment and lower turnover. 8. Summary Table AspectKey Point MeaningUnique psychological characteristics influencing behavior. FormationShaped by heredity, environment, and situation.

Extroverts thrive in sales; introverts in analytical roles.) 2.Person–Organization Fit: 20IMCAT422- OB_ Module 2 9 Dr. Sunandha Rajagopal When an individual’s values align with the organization’s culture, they show stronger commitment and lower turnover. 8. Summary Table AspectKey Point MeaningUnique psychological characteristics influencing behavior. FormationShaped by heredity, environment, and situation. Determinant s Biological, social, and cultural influences. TraitsBig Five: Extraversion, Agreeableness, Conscientiousness, Emotional Stability, Openness. AttributesLocus of control, self-esteem, Machiavellianism, self-monitoring, etc. OB Influence Affects performance, satisfaction, motivation, leadership, and teamwork. Process of perception - Factors influencing perception In every organization, employees constantly receive information from their environment — through people, events, and work situations. However, not everyone interprets this information the same way. This is because people perceive things differently based on their past experiences, needs, emotions, and expectations. According to Jerald Greenberg, “Perception is the process by which individuals organize and interpret their sensory impressions to give meaning to their environment.” Stephen P. Robbins adds that perception is important because: “People’s behaviour is based on their perception of what reality is, not on reality itself.” In simple words, perception is how people see and understand the world around them — and this understanding directly affects their attitude and behaviour at work. 2. Meaning of Perception Perception is a mental and psychological process through which an individual selects, organizes, and interprets information from the environment to form a meaningful picture of the world. It acts like a filter — we do not see everything objectively; we see what our mind allows or focuses on. Therefore, in an organizational context, perception influences communication, motivation, leadership, and decision-making. 3. The Process of Perception Perception is not a single-step activity. It is a continuous and dynamic process involving several stages. The main steps are: Stage 1: Sensory Input (Receiving Stimuli) 20IMCAT422- OB_ Module 2 10 Dr. Sunandha Rajagopal This is the first stage of perception where a person becomes aware of stimuli through the five senses — sight, sound, touch, taste, and smell. Since the environment provides too much information, individuals selectively pay attention to what seems important or relevant. Example: An employee notices a manager’s tone during feedback but may ignore background noise. Stage 2: Selection (Attention and Screening) We cannot pay attention to all stimuli. Therefore, the mind filters and selects only certain information for further processing. This selection depends on factors such as interest, need, and expectation. Example: A salesperson focuses more on customer reactions than on what others in the store are doing. Stage 3: Organization (Arranging Information) The selected information is then organized into patterns or categories to make sense of it. The brain groups similar things together based on shape, size, colour, or past experiences. This step follows the principles of Gestalt psychology, which states that “people tend to perceive objects as whole patterns rather than separate parts.” Example: When seeing several people talking together, you may perceive them as a team. Stage 4: Interpretation (Assigning Meaning) In this stage, the perceiver interprets the organized information

step follows the principles of Gestalt psychology, which states that “people tend to perceive objects as whole patterns rather than separate parts.” Example: When seeing several people talking together, you may perceive them as a team. Stage 4: Interpretation (Assigning Meaning) In this stage, the perceiver interprets the organized information and assigns meaning to it based on their knowledge, beliefs, emotions, and cultural background. This is where subjective judgment occurs. Example: One employee may interpret a manager’s strict tone as discipline, another as rudeness. Stage 5: Response (Behaviour or Reaction) Finally, perception leads to a response or action. The person reacts according to the meaning they have given to the situation. Example: If an employee perceives the manager’s feedback as constructive, they may work harder. If they perceive it as criticism, they may become defensive. Summary – Process of Perception StageDescriptionExample 1. Sensory InputReceiving stimuli from environment.Hearing manager’s voice. 2. SelectionPaying attention to relevant information.Noticing tone or words. 3. OrganizationGrouping and arranging information.Recognizing emotion in the voice. 4. Interpretation Assigning meaning based on experience. Deciding if tone means anger or urgency. 5. ResponseActing based on perceived meaning.Responding politely or defensively. 4. Factors Influencing Perception Perception is shaped by a combination of personal, situational, and target-related factors. As Stephen Robbins explains, three variables influence perception: The Perceiver (who sees), The Target (what is seen), and The Situation (the context). A. Factors in the Perceiver (Individual Factors) 20IMCAT422- OB_ Module 2 11 Dr. Sunandha Rajagopal The characteristics of the person perceiving have a strong impact on how they interpret situations. 1.Attitudes: A positive or negative attitude affects how one views others. Example: A manager with a positive outlook may see subordinates’ mistakes as learning opportunities. 2.Motives and Needs: People tend to perceive what satisfies their needs. Example: A person who needs recognition may interpret neutral feedback as praise. 3.Experience and Learning: Past experiences shape how we interpret new events. Example: An employee who had a harsh boss before may misread any strict manager as unfair. 4.Personality: Personality traits such as optimism, anxiety, or confidence affect perception. Example: Extroverts perceive teamwork as fun; introverts may see it as pressure. 5.Expectations: We see what we expect to see. Example: A teacher expecting good performance from a student may overlook small mistakes. 6.Emotional State: Mood affects how we perceive others. A stressed person might interpret neutral behaviour as hostility. B. Factors in the Target (Object Being Perceived) Characteristics of the object or person being observed can influence perception. 1.Novelty: Unusual or new things attract attention quickly. 2.Movement: Moving objects are noticed more than stationary ones. 3.Size and Intensity: Bigger or louder stimuli get more attention. 4.Background and Contrast: Objects that stand out against their background are easily noticed. 5.Status and Attractiveness: High-status or attractive people often receive more attention and are perceived more positively. Example: In meetings, people may pay more attention to senior managers than junior staff. C. Factors in the Situation (Contextual Factors) The context or environment in which perception occurs also affects

are easily noticed. 5.Status and Attractiveness: High-status or attractive people often receive more attention and are perceived more positively. Example: In meetings, people may pay more attention to senior managers than junior staff. C. Factors in the Situation (Contextual Factors) The context or environment in which perception occurs also affects how information is interpreted. 1.Time: Perception may vary depending on when it happens. Example: A tired employee at the end of the day may misread a casual remark as criticism. 2.Work Setting: The physical and social environment influences perception. Example: A relaxed office atmosphere encourages positive perceptions. 3.Social Context: People’s behaviour is perceived differently in groups than alone. Example: A manager’s joke in a meeting may be viewed as friendly, but the same comment in private may seem inappropriate. 5. Perceptual Errors in Organizations Because of these influencing factors, perception often becomes biased or inaccurate. 20IMCAT422- OB_ Module 2 12 Dr. Sunandha Rajagopal Some common perceptual distortions include: ErrorDescriptionExample StereotypingJudging someone based on group membership.“Women are emotional,” “Older workers resist change.” Halo EffectForming an overall impression based on one trait. Assuming a punctual person is also efficient. Selective Perception Seeing only what fits our beliefs.Ignoring feedback that disagrees with our views. ProjectionAttributing one’s feelings to others.A dishonest person suspects everyone else lies. Contrast EffectComparing one person’s performance to another’s instead of objective standards. Rating a good employee poorly after seeing an excellent one. 6. Importance of Perception in Organizational Behaviour Understanding perception is vital for managers because it influences almost all aspects of human behaviour at work: 1.Communication: Helps in sending clear and accurate messages. 2.Decision-Making: Managers make fair judgments when they understand perceptual biases. 3.Leadership: Leaders who understand perception can motivate effectively. 4.Conflict Resolution: Correcting misperceptions reduces misunderstandings. 5.Performance Appraisal: Avoids biased or unfair evaluations. 6.Teamwork: Improves mutual understanding and trust among team members. Foundations of Individual Behaviour Organizations are made up of people, and their success depends largely on how these people behave at work. To understand Organizational Behaviour (OB), we must first understand the individual — what drives, influences, and shapes their actions. According to Stephen P. Robbins, “The study of individual behaviour in organizations helps us understand why people behave the way they do and how we can motivate them to perform better.” Jerald Greenberg adds that understanding individuals is the first step in managing human behaviour effectively in an organization. Thus, the foundations of individual behaviour refer to the basic elements — both internal and external — that determine how a person behaves within a work environment. 2. Meaning of Individual Behaviour 20IMCAT422- OB_ Module 2 13 Dr. Sunandha Rajagopal Individual behaviour means how a person reacts to different situations and interacts with others in an organization. It is the result of a combination of personal characteristics, organizational context, and environmental factors. In simple terms, it is “what a person does at work and how they do it.” 3. Elements (Foundations) of Individual Behaviour Individual behaviour is influenced by several basic elements or foundations. The

an organization. It is the result of a combination of personal characteristics, organizational context, and environmental factors. In simple terms, it is “what a person does at work and how they do it.” 3. Elements (Foundations) of Individual Behaviour Individual behaviour is influenced by several basic elements or foundations. The major ones are: A. Biographical Characteristics These are personal background factors that affect behaviour, such as age, gender, marital status, experience, and education. FactorInfluence on Behaviour AgeOlder employees tend to have more experience, are more cautious and loyal; younger employees are more energetic and adaptable. GenderDifferences may exist in communication, emotional expression, or leadership style, but both can perform equally well when given equal opportunities. Marital StatusMarried employees often have higher stability and fewer absences due to greater responsibility. Tenure / Experience Longer tenure usually leads to higher job satisfaction and better understanding of organizational culture. EducationHigher education can improve analytical skills, creativity, and openness to change. B. Ability Ability refers to an individual’s capacity to perform tasks. It has two main types: 1.Intellectual Ability – Mental capacity such as reasoning, memory, and problem-solving. Example: Analytical thinking, numerical ability, and verbal comprehension. 2.Physical Ability – Physical strength, stamina, and dexterity required for certain jobs. Example: Construction workers need physical strength; software engineers rely on mental ability. Matching a person’s ability with the job’s requirements leads to higher performance and satisfaction. C. Personality Personality is the set of psychological traits that make individuals unique. It influences how people think, feel, and act at work. Common personality dimensions include:  Extraversion  Agreeableness  Conscientiousness  Emotional Stability  Openness to Experience These traits (known as the Big Five Model) influence communication, leadership, teamwork, and motivation. 20IMCAT422- OB_ Module 2 14 Dr. Sunandha Rajagopal Example: A conscientious person is disciplined and performs tasks systematically; an extrovert performs better in social or team-oriented roles. D. Perception Perception is the process by which people interpret and give meaning to information from their environment. It explains why two individuals facing the same situation may react differently.  Influenced by personal experiences, motives, and expectations.  Affects decision-making, communication, and relationships in the workplace. Example: An employee may perceive strict supervision as guidance, while another may see it as lack of trust. E. Attitudes Attitude refers to a person’s favourable or unfavourable evaluation toward something — a job, colleague, or organization. The three components of attitude are: 1.Cognitive (beliefs) – “My job is interesting.” 2.Affective (feelings) – “I enjoy my job.” 3.Behavioural (actions) – “I work hard and stay loyal.” Work-related attitudes such as job satisfaction, organizational commitment, and engagement are major predictors of employee behaviour. F. Motivation Motivation is the inner drive that directs an individual’s behaviour toward achieving goals. It answers “why people work hard or avoid effort.” Motivation is influenced by:  Needs (Maslow’s hierarchy – physiological to self-actualization)  Expectancy of rewards (Vroom’s Expectancy Theory)  Fairness and equity (Adams’ Equity Theory) Motivated employees show enthusiasm, commitment, and better performance. G. Learning

individual’s behaviour toward achieving goals. It answers “why people work hard or avoid effort.” Motivation is influenced by:  Needs (Maslow’s hierarchy – physiological to self-actualization)  Expectancy of rewards (Vroom’s Expectancy Theory)  Fairness and equity (Adams’ Equity Theory) Motivated employees show enthusiasm, commitment, and better performance. G. Learning Learning is a relatively permanent change in behaviour resulting from experience or practice. People learn from:  Observation (watching others),  Reinforcement (rewards and punishments),  Experience (successes and failures). Fred Luthans highlights that behaviour can be modified using positive reinforcement — rewarding desired actions to encourage repetition. H. Values Values are the core beliefs that guide behaviour and decision-making. They define what is right or wrong, important or unimportant. Types: 20IMCAT422- OB_ Module 2 15 Dr. Sunandha Rajagopal  Terminal values: Desired end states (e.g., happiness, success, freedom).  Instrumental values: Preferred ways of behaving (e.g., honesty, hard work, loyalty). Values influence ethical behaviour, goal setting, and cultural alignment. I. Emotions and Emotional Intelligence (EI) Emotions are intense feelings directed toward something or someone. They can be positive (joy, excitement) or negative (anger, anxiety). Emotional Intelligence (EI)—the ability to recognize, understand, and manage one’s own emotions and those of others—is crucial in the workplace. Employees with high EI are:  Better communicators,  More empathetic and cooperative,  Effective in leadership and conflict resolution. J. Organizational Factors Apart from personal traits, certain organizational elements also affect individual behaviour:  Work Environment: Physical and psychological conditions.  Leadership Style: Supportive leadership boosts morale.  Organizational Culture: Shared values influence norms and ethics.  Reward System: Fair rewards enhance motivation and job satisfaction.  Supervision and Relationships: Healthy interactions improve cooperation and performance. 4. Interaction of Individual and Organizational Factors Individual behaviour is the result of the interaction between personal and organizational factors. Behaviour = f (Person × Environment) (Behaviour is a function of the person and their environment.) Thus, even the same person may behave differently in different organizational settings depending on culture, leadership, and structure. 5. Importance of Understanding Individual Behaviour in Organizations ImportanceExplanation 1. Better ManagementHelps managers understand employee needs and expectations. 2. Improved CommunicationUnderstanding perceptions and attitudes reduces misunderstandings. 3. Enhanced MotivationManagers can design appropriate incentives. 4. Effective LeadershipHelps leaders adapt styles to different personalities. 5. Conflict ResolutionIdentifying differences helps in preventing and solving disputes. 6. Job Satisfaction & Matching abilities and roles increases efficiency. 20IMCAT422- OB_ Module 2 16 Dr. Sunandha Rajagopal Performance 7. Organizational HarmonyPromotes teamwork and positive workplace culture. Skills in Organizational Behaviour In the study of Organizational Behaviour (OB), skills refer to the learned abilities that enable individuals to perform effectively within an organizational setting. These skills form the foundation for understanding, predicting, and influencing behavior at individual, group, and organizational levels. They contribute directly to productivity, collaboration, leadership, and job satisfaction. Organizational behaviour identifies several categories of skills necessary for professional effectiveness: intellectual skills, social skills, personal skills, expert skills, and technical skills. 1. Intellectual Skills Meaning: Intellectual skills involve cognitive capabilities

and influencing behavior at individual, group, and organizational levels. They contribute directly to productivity, collaboration, leadership, and job satisfaction. Organizational behaviour identifies several categories of skills necessary for professional effectiveness: intellectual skills, social skills, personal skills, expert skills, and technical skills. 1. Intellectual Skills Meaning: Intellectual skills involve cognitive capabilities that enable an individual to think critically, analyze situations, and make informed decisions. They are essential for understanding complex organizational dynamics, interpreting data, and solving problems. Description: According to Greenberg (2004) and Luthans (2006), intellectual skills form the foundation for managerial effectiveness and problem-solving. They include abilities such as logical reasoning, analytical thinking, creativity, and decision-making. Key Components:  Analytical ability: Breaking down complex problems into manageable parts.  Critical thinking: Evaluating information objectively before forming judgments.  Strategic thinking: Anticipating long-term implications and outcomes.  Decision-making: Choosing optimal solutions among alternatives based on evidence.  Cognitive flexibility: Adapting thoughts and strategies according to changing circumstances. Example: A manager analyzing employee performance data to identify productivity issues and suggesting corrective training programs demonstrates strong intellectual skills. 2. Social Skills Meaning: Social skills refer to the ability to interact effectively with others in an organization. They encompass communication, empathy, cooperation, and emotional intelligence. Description: Robbins (2006) emphasizes that social skills are crucial for teamwork, conflict resolution, and leadership. These skills determine how well individuals relate to others and influence behavior through interpersonal understanding. Key Components:  Communication: The ability to express ideas clearly and listen actively.  Empathy: Understanding others’ emotions and perspectives.  Interpersonal relationship management: Building and maintaining positive work relationships. 20IMCAT422- OB_ Module 2 17 Dr. Sunandha Rajagopal  Negotiation: Resolving disagreements and reaching mutual agreements.  Teamwork: Cooperating and collaborating toward shared goals. Example: A project leader who mediates conflicts within the team and motivates members to work collaboratively exhibits strong social skills. 3. Personal Skills Meaning: Personal skills refer to self-management abilities that influence how individuals perceive themselves and their work environment. These include emotional stability, self-awareness, adaptability, and self-motivation. Description: Luthans (2006) links personal skills with emotional intelligence and self-regulation — essential traits for coping with organizational stress, maintaining discipline, and achieving consistent performance. Key Components:  Self-awareness: Understanding one’s strengths, weaknesses, and emotions.  Self-control: Managing impulses and maintaining composure under pressure.  Resilience: Bouncing back from setbacks and maintaining a positive attitude.  Adaptability: Adjusting behavior and mindset according to organizational changes.  Motivation: Maintaining enthusiasm and commitment toward work goals. Example: An employee who remains calm during organizational restructuring and continues to perform efficiently shows strong personal skills. 4. Expert Skills Meaning: Expert skills are domain-specific abilities that stem from deep knowledge and experience in a particular area of work. They represent the professional expertise that differentiates specialists from generalists. Description: According to Robbins and Greenberg, expert skills enable individuals to provide valuable insights, solve specialized problems, and guide others in technical or strategic domains. Key Components:  Domain expertise: Mastery of a particular subject or field.  Problem-solving in specialization: Applying

the professional expertise that differentiates specialists from generalists. Description: According to Robbins and Greenberg, expert skills enable individuals to provide valuable insights, solve specialized problems, and guide others in technical or strategic domains. Key Components:  Domain expertise: Mastery of a particular subject or field.  Problem-solving in specialization: Applying advanced knowledge to unique challenges.  Advisory ability: Offering professional guidance or mentoring others.  Continuous learning: Updating knowledge to stay relevant in the field. Example: A financial analyst who interprets market data and advises management on investment strategies demonstrates expert skills. 5. Technical Skills Meaning: Technical skills involve proficiency in using specific tools, techniques, and procedures necessary to perform particular tasks. These skills are often learned through training and experience. 20IMCAT422- OB_ Module 2 18 Dr. Sunandha Rajagopal Description: Fred Luthans (2006) defines technical skills as job-specific competencies that are particularly critical at lower and middle levels of management. They include understanding and applying methods, processes, and technologies required to perform organizational functions effectively. Key Components:  Operational proficiency: Ability to use machinery, software, or systems effectively.  Procedural knowledge: Understanding organizational processes and standards.  Data handling: Using tools for analysis, documentation, and reporting.  Technological adaptability: Learning and implementing new technologies relevant to the job. Example: An IT specialist proficient in database management systems and network troubleshooting demonstrates strong technical skills.
